question.text: What is the Black-Scholes model used for in derivative markets?

answer.text: It is a mathematical model for pricing European-style options, using five inputs: spot price, strike price, time to expiry, volatility, and risk-free rate to compute the theoretical fair value of an option
It is a model for pricing futures contracts based on cost-of-carry
It is used only for pricing commodity options, not financial options
It calculates the margin required for derivative positions

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Black-Scholes (1973) provides a closed-form solution for European option pricing: C = S×N(d1) - K×e^(-rT)×N(d2), P = K×e^(-rT)×N(-d2) - S×N(-d1). Key assumptions: constant volatility, no dividends (basic version), lognormal price distribution, continuous trading. **The Trap:** Black-Scholes is for options, not futures. It applies to financial options broadly, not just commodities. It's a pricing model, not a margin model. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch4 covers Black-Scholes for equity options. Modified versions (Garman-Kohlhagen for FX, Black-76 for IR) are used in Series I and IV. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** While the model has known limitations (assumes constant volatility, no jumps), it remains the market standard for quoting implied volatility — all option traders use it as a common language.
